coredata.dc:title: Excessive acid reagent-induced luminescent blue shift: A strategy for synthesizing full-color carbon dots and its application in information encryption and multicolor LEDs
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Huajiang
Li, Jiurong
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Due to their unique fluorescence properties and wide range of applications, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a prominent research subject. Acid reagents have traditionally been regarded as versatile means to facilitate the carbonization and polymerization of CDs, resulting in increased carbonization and polymerization levels, which in turn cause a redshift in the luminescence of CDs. However, there has been limited investigation into the impact of excess acid reagents on CDs luminescence. In this study, we discovered that the addition of excess acid reagent, in the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, does not enhance the graphitization of CDs, but rather, it has the opposite effect. Excess acid reagents prevent CDs polymerization and reduce the content of graphite-N in the CDs. Simultaneously, excess acid reagent leads to an increase in oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs, resulting in a blueshift in luminescence. Building upon these findings, we have devised a synthesis scheme for full-color CDs that cover nearly the entire visible light spectrum. Furthermore, leveraging the diverse color range, we have also employed these CDs for anti-counterfeiting encryption and LEDs applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Full color
Acid regulation
Fluorescent inks
Multicolor LEDs

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process and application of CDs.

(a) Photos of the as-prepared CDs in daylight (upper) and UV light (bottom), respectively. (b) Absorption spectra of B-CDs to R-CDs. (c) Excitation spectra of B-CDs to R-CDs. (d) PL spectra of B-CDs to R-CDs at the optimal excitation wavelength. (e) Lifetime decay curves of B-CDs to R-CDs. (f) Energy level of B-CDs to R-CDs.
TEM images of (a) B-CDs, (b) C-CDs, (c) G-CDs, (d) Y-CDs, (e) P-CDs and (f) R-CDs. Insets in (a-f) are the corresponding HRTEM images (upper right) and size distribution histograms (lower right).

(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD patterns and (c) Full XPS spectra of B-CDs to R-CDs; High-resolution C1s (d), N1s (e) and O1s (f) spectrum of B-CDs to R-CDs; (g) Schematic diagram of CDs at emission wavelengths controlled by Graphitic-N and size.
(a) CDs alcohol solution for stamps. (b) CDs ink for painting. (c) CDs ink for advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption.
Spectra of LEDs (a) and CIE coordinates (b).

head.ce:title: Excessive acid reagent-induced luminescent blue shift: A strategy for synthesizing full-color carbon dots and its application in information encryption and multicolor LEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their unique fluorescence properties and wide range of applications, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a prominent research subject. Acid reagents have traditionally been regarded as versatile means to facilitate the carbonization and polymerization of CDs, resulting in increased carbonization and polymerization levels, which in turn cause a redshift in the luminescence of CDs. However, there has been limited investigation into the impact of excess acid reagents on CDs luminescence. In this study, we discovered that the addition of excess acid reagent, in the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, does not enhance the graphitization of CDs, but rather, it has the opposite effect. Excess acid reagents prevent CDs polymerization and reduce the content of graphite-N in the CDs. Simultaneously, excess acid reagent leads to an increase in oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs, resulting in a blueshift in luminescence. Building upon these findings, we have devised a synthesis scheme for full-color CDs that cover nearly the entire visible light spectrum. Furthermore, leveraging the diverse color range, we have also employed these CDs for anti-counterfeiting encryption and LEDs applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging carbon-based luminescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have received extensive research interest due to their many specific properties, such as its excellent photo-stability, remarkable biocompatibility, and unique emission tuning properties, which offer interesting potential applications in optoelectronic devices, photo-catalysis, human eye protection, catalytic, information encryption, bio-imaging, etc. [1–13]. Currently, two main top-down and bottom-up pathways are employed for the synthesis of CDs using a large number of organic carbon sources and green biomass precursors [14–16]. However, most of the synthesized CDs show blue emission with high quantum efficiency if the synthesis method is not improved. Multicolor tuning is one of the most attractive properties of CDs. Still, the emission of CDs depends greatly on the synthesis process, raw materials, reaction time, temperature, solvent, etc. [17–25]. Notably, most of the synthesized multicolor CDs display blue/green/yellow emission and the absence of red emission region limits their applicability in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [26,27]. Recently, several research groups have synthesized CDs with full spectral emission from blue to red emission and applied them in various fields, especially in multicolor LEDs and multicolor displays [28–33].

For a long time, the addition of acid reagents has been considered a common strategy for assisting the synthesis of CDs. Extensive research has shown that the addition of acid reagents can promote the polymerization of CDs and enhance their carbonization degree, often resulting in a redshift of the emission wavelength [34–42]. Therefore, acid reagents are commonly used means for preparing long-wavelength CDs. For example, Xian et al. [36] suggested that the addition of acid facilitates dehydration, carbonization, and the conversion from pyridinic nitrogen to pyrrolic nitrogen, based on which they synthesized highly efficient red-emitting CDs with ultra-narrow emission. Ma et al. [34] achieved a significant redshift of CDs by adding hydrochloric acid (HCl). This is because the protonation of amino groups in CDs caused by the addition of HCl promotes their aggregation, resulting in a redshift of the emission wavelength. Hence, acid reagents are widely employed to modulate the emission wavelength of CDs. Based on this, numerous studies on multicolor CDs often choose to add acid reagents to synthesize long-wavelength CDs [15,34,39,41,42]. However, there have been few studies on the blue shift of emission wavelength induced by the addition of acid reagents, and the influence of excessive addition of acid reagents on the luminescence of CDs is often neglected.

In this work, we designed an acid-assisted strategy to synthesize long-wavelength CDs. When we attempted to increase the amount of acid reagent, significant blue shifts in the emission wavelength were observed. We found that the excessive addition of acid reagents, in the presence of oxygen-functional groups, not only did not increase the polymerization degree of CDs and redshift the emission wavelength but instead hindered the polymerization of CDs and reduced the content of graphite-N in CDs. Moreover, the excessive addition of acid reagents increased the surface oxygen-functional groups, resulting in a blue shift of the emission wavelength. Based on this, we designed and synthesized full-color CDs with emission wavelengths ranging from 397 nm to 650 nm. The fluorescent properties of full-color CDs can be used to prepare invisible fluorescent ink for advanced information security encryption. With their excellent optical properties, full-color CDs also have promising applications in the field of LEDs (Scheme 1 ).

o-phenylenediamine (oPD), m-aminophenol (mAP), polyvinylpyrrolidone (K30 PVP), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), and hydroxypropyl cellulose were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. hydrochloric acid (HCl), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), toluene (TOL), acetone (ACE), dichloromethane (DCM), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.

The synthesis process was carried out by a one-step hydrothermal reaction of the precursors at 180 °C for 12 h. For simplicity, the corresponding different fluorescence emissions (blue, cyan, green, yellow, pink and red) were named as blue CDs (B-CDs), cyan CDs (C-CDs), green CDs (G-CDs), yellow CDs (Y-CDs), pink CDs (P-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs). A volume of 10 mL was maintained throughout the experiment. For the synthesis of R-CDs, briefly, 1 mmol oPD and 1 mmol mAP were dissolved in 9.5 mL of deionized water. After the reactants were dissolved, 0.5 mL of HCl was added to it, and the mixture was subsequently transferred to an autoclave (25 mL) with a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) liner under 180°C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged to extract the precipitate, which was then washed three to five times with deionized water, and finally, the precipitate was dried in an oven at 65 °C for subsequent characterization. As for the synthesis of P-CDs, the synthesis process was identical to that of R-CDs, except that 0.5 mL of HCl was replaced with 1 mL of H3PO4, while the synthesis process of Y-CDs was also similar to that of R-CDs, except that the dose of 0.5 mL of HCl was replaced with 4 mL. However, as for B-CDs, C-CDs and G-CDs, the supernatant of the reaction mixture was extracted from all three. As for B-CDs, the specific synthesis procedure was the same as that for P-CDs, except that the dose of 1 mL H3PO4 was replaced with 2 mL, while for G-CDs, the dose of H3PO4 was replaced with 0.5 mL and the solvent was substituted with EtOH, and other conditions were the same as those for P-CDs. Similarly, C-CDs were synthesized under the same conditions as G-CDs, except that H3PO4 was replaced with HCl. To better compare the effect of acid in the experiments, the control CDs were prepared by direct reaction in deionized water, and the supernatant was taken in the same way and recorded as CD0. It is worth noting that all reaction supernatants were dialyzed through a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da for 24 h. Finally, the dialysate was treated by rotary evaporation.

Fluorescent ink is prepared by mixing hydroxypropyl cellulose with CDs. In simple terms, 0.5 g of hydroxypropyl cellulose is added to 10 mL of EtOH solution of CDs and then stirred until the hydroxypropyl cellulose is completely dissolved.

The CDs/PVP mixture was obtained by mixing 0.1 g of K30 PVP with 0.5 mL of CDs solution, stirring for 10 min, and then homogenizing the mixture after ultrasonic treatment. For the fabrication of monochromatic LEDs, the CDs/PVP composites were integrated onto InGaN LED chips. Finally, these LEDs were placed directly into the oven and cured at 65°C for 1 h.

120 KV transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-1400Plus/JEM-1400Plus) was used for morphology and structure characterization. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi/ESCALAB 250Xi) was utilized to elemental and compositional analysis. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) was applied for lattice structure analysis. Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (Nicolet6700/Nicolet6700) were utilized for composition and structure analysis. UV–visible near-infrared spectrophotometer (Lambda 750 S/Lambda 750 S) was used to characterize the optical properties. The PL spectra of the LEDs were characterized by a Spectrascan Model PR-650 spectrophotometer with an integral sphere. The quantum yields (QY) were characterized by the Edinburgh FLS1000 device.

The full-color CDs with fluorescence ranging from blue to red were synthesized by an acid reagent-assisted synthetic strategy, i.e., a one-step hydrothermal approach with the assistance of HCl and H3PO4. As presented in Fig. 1a , the as-synthesized full-color CDs exhibited a bright fluorescent color under a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating that the acid reagent-assisted synthesis strategy is an effective synthetic method for fluorescence emission regulation of CDs. To further evaluate the optical properties of the full-color CDs, UV absorption, excitation and emission of fluorescence and fluorescence lifetime were applied to characterize B-CDs, C-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, P-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. Additionally, a blank control group experiment without the participation of acid reagents in the synthesis was employed to better highlight the key role of acid reagents, named CD0. Fig. 1b presents the UV absorption spectra of the full-color CDs, and it can be clearly observed that the absorption bands of the six CDs could be roughly divided into three regions, i.e., 200–250 nm, 250–350 nm and 350–650 nm. Firstly, the primary absorption peak at the 200–250 nm band was located at about 235 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds or CN bonds in CDs [43,44]. Secondly, the main absorption peak at the 250–350 nm band was located at approximately 280 nm, which can be originated from the n-π* transitions of heterocyclic, CO, or CN groups in CDs [44]. While the absorption in the range of 350–650 nm band may be derived from some other functional groups on the surface of CDs, which is beneficial to reduce the band gap of the fluorescent material [4,45]. Notably, with the increase of fluorescence emission wavelength, the absorption at 235 nm showed a gradual weakening trend, while the absorption located at 280 nm showed a significant enhancement, which may be originated from the increase in the size of the sp2 structural domain in the core [46]. Furthermore, the electronic energy levels and lifetimes of CDs will also alter as the emission wavelength increases, leading to different behaviors of the absorption peaks at different wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 1c, different excitation wavelengths of the full-color CDs were demonstrated, corresponding to the optimal excitation wavelengths of 331 nm, 395 nm, 451 nm, 408 nm, 535 nm and 590 nm, respectively. As the fluorescence emission wavelength increased, the corresponding excitation wavelength also increased. Surprisingly, an anomaly occurred for Y-CDs, which does not follow this variation rule but instead shows a blue shift of excitation wavelength on top of it. This may be attributed to the fact that Y-CDs have different surface or molecular states. Accordingly, the best emission spectrum corresponding to the full-color CDs is displayed in Fig. 1d. It can be clearly observed that the full-color fluorescence emission wavelengths from blue to red (397 nm, 483 nm, 516 nm, 555 nm, 593 nm and 650 nm, respectively) could be synthesized with the aid of acid reagents, yielding an overall red shift of almost 253 nm. To demonstrate the key role of the acid reagent in the synthesis process, the PL spectra of CD0 were compared. As depicted in Figure S1, the solution of CD0 appeared blue-white under UV lamp without the participation of acid reagent, and the PL spectra corresponding to different excitations also showed that the emission spectrum of CD0 ranged from 380–650 nm when the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, which covered almost the whole UV-visible region, which also indicated that CD0 was enriched with fluorescent components from blue to red. It also can be observed that CD0 exhibited multiple emission peaks in EtOH solvent and there was a clear excitation dependence phenomenon, indicating the presence of multiple fluorescence sources in the CDs without acid addition, probably from different luminescent CDs [47]. The addition of acid reagent can precisely enhance a certain fluorescence component in CD0, which indicates the essential role of acid reagent in this synthesis strategy. We also studied the effect of different amounts of HCl and H3PO4 added when alcohol was used as the reaction solvent, and found that their luminescence remained largely unchanged (Figure S2). Additionally, the time-resolved PL analyses were performed to obtain additional information on the bandgap emission characteristics of the full-color CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1e. The PL decay curves of full-color CDs were fitted by double exponential curves and the specific relevant values are shown in Table S1. The results revealed that the average fluorescence lifetimes of the full-color CDs were 6.15 ns, 4.51 ns, 6.60 ns, 2.79 ns, 2.37 ns and 1.26 ns, respectively. It is obvious that the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes were progressively shorter with the red shift of the fluorescence emission wavelength. The decreased lifetime of CDs implies that the recombination pathway by the core state and the larger sp2 structural domain induce faster fluorescence decay [39,48,49]. As demonstrated in Fig. 1f, the bandgap energy of the full-color CDs from B-CDs to R-CDs were calculated to be from approximately 3.12 eV to 1.91 eV, respectively, by exploiting the equation
E g
o p t
= 1240 /
λ
e d g e
[18,50–52]. The decrease in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level may result from the increase in the size of the CDs, which in turn produces a decrease in the band gap, due to the fact that increasing the size of the CDs corresponds to an increase in the graphitization or conjugation length, leading to a decrease in the band gap and ultimately a red shift in the emission wavelength [53,54].

To better understand the properties of CDs, a systematic study was conducted on the excitation dependence, concentration effect and solvent effect of all CDs. (Figure S3 to Figure S7). It was found experimentally that all other CDs exhibited good excitation independence (Figure S3), i.e., their emission peaks remained unchanged when the excitation wavelength was changed. This indicated that these CDs had relatively stable emission centers, and these emission centers had some adaptability to different excitation wavelengths. For the blue CDs, it can be found that there were obvious differences in their emission centers. At different excitation wavelengths, the emission peaks of the blue CDs showed different positions and intensity changes, indicating that they had different emission centers and these emission centers dominated in different ways under different excitations [55]. In addition, the effect of concentration on the optical properties of the CDs was also investigated (Figure S4 and Figure S5), and it can be seen that all the CDs except the R-CDs did not show any significant peak shift at different concentrations. The R-CDs showed a significant weakening of the peak at 650 nm when the concentration was decreased, while a new peak gradually strengthened at nearly 600 nm. This may be due to the aggregation-induced emission of R-CDs caused by the different sizes of aggregates formed by R-CDs at different concentrations, which may be pure in highly ordered large particle structures in highly concentrated R-CDs solutions [56]. To investigate the solvent dispersion and corresponding fluorescence properties of the panchromatic CDs, all CDs were dissolved in different organic solvents as shown in Figure S6 and Figure S7. As shown in Figure S7, the PL spectra in different solvents were slightly different at the optimal excitation wavelength, but all showed a red-shifted trend, which may be due to the different surface states, i.e., as the solvent polarity increases, the dipole moment has a stronger effect on the surface electronic structure of CDs, which leads to a decrease in band gap as well as a change in PL emission [4,57,58]. Finally, we tested the QY of B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, and the results of the tests are shown in Table S2, where they show QY ranging from 8.70 % to 23.66 %.

The microscopic morphology and structure of the full color CDs were characterized by TEM and-high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM), as illustrated in Fig. 2 . From Fig. 2a to f, it is obvious that all the CDs had excellent dispersion and all exhibited quasi-spherical structures. More importantly, all the CDs presented distinct lattice fringes and the lattice fringe spacing was mainly 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the [100] surface of graphite [59–61]. The overall particle sizes distributions were statistically analyzed based on the distribution of CDs in the TEM. The average particle sizes of B-CDs, C-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, P-CDs and R-CDs were 2.22 nm, 2.19 nm, 2.60 nm, 4.13 nm, 4.84 nm and 5.09 nm, respectively. It is worth noting that the particle size distribution results indicated that the size of CDs increased with the red shift of fluorescence emission wavelength, which may be one of the main factors causing the red shift of emission wavelength, and indicating that there was a significant quantum size effect of the CDs [62]. This is consistent with the conjecture of the analytical results of PL.

To more effectively analyze the structure, functional group components and elemental components contained in the CDs, the synthesized CDs were characterized with the assistance of FT-IR, XRD and XPS. The surface components of the CDs were also investigated by FT-IR (Fig. 3a ). The FT-IR spectra of the CDs indicated stretching vibrations corresponding to OH/NH2 (3300–3620 cm−1), CH (2925 cm−1), CO/CN (1620 cm−1), CN (1475 cm−1), CC (1218 cm−1) and CO (1130 cm−1) [17,42,63–66]. Additionally, the peaks at near 753 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibrations of the CH of the benzene ring [67]. These results were demonstrated that the presence of CN and CC stretching bonds confirmed the presence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in CDs [23,68,69]. Furthermore, it can also be seen that the CN and CN bonds showed a significant increase from B-CDs to R-CDs, which indicated a consequent increase in the nitrogen (N) contents of the CDs, and might be one of the factors causing the red shift of the CDs fluorescence emission (e.g., increase in graphitic N) [70]. The XRD measurements were applied to characterize the graphitic structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, a broad peak can be clearly observed near 23°, which can be attributed to the [002] plane in graphitic carbon. Besides, a weak diffraction peak was also present nearby 43°, which can be ascribed to the highly disordered carbon present in the CDs, which can correspond to the [100] plane of graphitic carbon [43,71–73]. These peaks well indicated the graphitic nature of the prepared CDs, which is in good agreement with HRTEM (Fig. 2). As illustrated in Fig. 3c, XPS full spectrum demonstrated that all CDs possessed three distinct characteristic peaks located at about 286.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 532.08 eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively [74]. The presence of N elements indicates their successful doping into all the CDs. Distinct variations among these three elements can be observed, with a significant increase in the content of carbon (C) and N as the CDs exhibit a redshift, while the content of oxygen (O) experiences a notable decrease (Table S3). To further investigate the possible chemical bonding forms of the C, N, and O elements in the CDs, high-resolution XPS analysis was employed for fitting. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s could be divided into three small peaks located at approximately 284.8 eV, 285.60 eV and 286.7 eV, which can be attributed to the presence of CC/CC, CO/CN and CO groups, respectively [75]. Clearly, the CC/CC group occupied the predominant role, which indicated the formation of sp2 conjugated domain and sp3 amorphous carbon in the CDs. Also, the CO group is clearly seen to display an increasing phenomenon with the red shift of the luminescence wavelength from the B-CDs to the R-CDs (Table S4). More importantly, the CO group can cause local segregation and strain and distortion of the carbon base plane, which can narrow the energy gap and eventually lead to PL red shift [53,76]. Similarly, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3e) could be decomposed into three peaks at near 398.5, 399.1, and 401.2 eV, which are ascribed to pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, respectively [53,77,78]. Most conspicuously, the graphite N content showed a clear increasing trend with the red shift of the luminescence wavelength from the B-CDs to the R-CDs (Table S5). Graphite nitrogen (N) doping has a significant effect on the HOMO-LUMO gap within carbon-based materials. By narrowing this energy difference, it leads to a reduction in the energy required for photon leaps. As a result, a strong redshift trend is observed in the resulting optical properties [70]. Besides, the pyridinic N content is significantly reduced, and the increase of pyridinic N content can lead to exciton trapping and change of surface state, resulting in the blue shift of fluorescence [4,79]. Fig. 3f shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s, which could be roughly separated into two peaks located at around 530.1 eV and 532.0 eV, corresponding to CO and CO, respectively [80]. It can be seen that the CO component had an increasing trend from B-CDs to R-CDs (Table S6). The relevant XPS full spectra and high-resolution XPS elemental analysis spectra of C-CDs and P-CDs are presented in Figure S9. Based on the analysis of the overall XPS results, it can be found that the results of C-CDs and P-CDs were slightly different. This is mainly due to the different solvents utilized in the reaction process. Based on the above analysis and previous reports, we speculate on the possible formation mode of CDs. 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) is formed by dehydration of oPD with the help of oxygen in an aqueous solvent, usually in the presence of oxygen in deionized water [75,81]. Under high temperature and pressure conditions, the several types of molecules formed will be cross-linked and carbonized to form highly crystalline carbon core [81]. In addition, due to the lower pH environment of the reaction solvent, which accelerates the ionization of NH2, the amine groups on the surface of the CDs are continuously depleted, and the fluorescent groups are converted from DAP via a deamidation reaction to 5,14-dihydroquinoxalino[2,3-b]phenazine (DHQP), which is attached to the surface of the carbon dots dominating the red light emission from the CDs [67,81]. In contrast, the increase of acid led to a decrease in the oxygen content of the solvent, which hindered the oPD dehydration process and allowed the dehydration between oPD and mAP to dominate. The formation of DAP by dehydration of oPD at the initial stage of the reaction has been reported to play an important role in the subsequent further polymerization of CDs to produce red emission [67]. In addition the strong acidic environment in the subsequent reaction may hinder the further polymerization of oPD, limiting the size of the CDs. Analysis of the whole XPS results showed that the graphite N component was accompanied by an increasing trend in the redshift of the fluorescence emission wavelength. Combined with the TEM results, this suggests that quantum size effects and changes in graphite N and oxygen content may be the main factors contributing to the shift in the emission of panchromatic CDs, and a schematic representation of the structure of potential CDs is depicted in Fig. 3g.

Due to the multi-color emission properties of the CDs, they can be applied to fluorescent inks. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 , fluorescent inks could be easily prepared by dispersing CDs in EtOH. By applying the CD's EtOH solution to the stamp, clear cartoon patterns could be printed on the filter paper (Fig. 4a). It can be seen that these cartoon patterns were difficult to distinguish under sunlight, but they could be easily distinguished under UV light. Meanwhile, to achieve the writing effect of fluorescent ink, we added a small amount of hydroxypropyl cellulose to the CDs solutions. As shown in Fig. 4b, the ink had good anti-counterfeiting and writing properties, and the patterns could be clearly seen under UV light, indicating that the prepared CDs have great potential in anti-counterfeiting. For this purpose, we have developed a secure encryption scheme based on the Morse code using the B-CDs, G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4c, by using blue and green discs to indicate "·"and "-" or "-" and "·" (depending on whether they are preceded by red or yellow). The figure shows the encrypted pattern, which could be deciphered as W, H, U, and T depending on the pre-designed result. We can easily design the encryption scheme due to the diversity of colors, while the combination of multiple colors can greatly increase the strength of the encryption and the difficulty of deciphering it.

Multicolor has always been a major feature in the field of CDs, which has led to a wide range of applications in optoelectronics and displays. Due to the excellent dispersibility and solubility of the synthesized full-color CDs, it can be easily compounded with some polymeric matrices (e.g., PVP). Using this superior property, multicolor LEDs on 365 nm InGaN chips were fabricated, as shown in Fig. 5a shows the electroluminescence (EL) spectrum of the multicolor LEDs with an operating current of 20 mA. It can be clearly seen that the optimal emission centers of the six monochromatic LEDs were located at about 425 nm, 495 nm, 533 nm, 558nm, 615 nm, and 650 nm, respectively. This is slightly offset from the PL spectrum of the original CDs solution, which may be due to the interaction of the CDs with PVP during the mixing process and the change in distance between the particles of the CDs as they move from solution to film. Since there is a degree of overlap between the absorption and emission spectra, as the particle spacing of the CDs becomes smaller, this causes a solid-state resonance energy transfer, which results in a relative enhancement of the emission at longer wavelengths [82]. Through the optical photos of multicolor LEDs under working conditions, it can also be seen that full-color CDs had good potential for LED applications, and could emit strong light under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet chip. As shown in Fig. 5b, the six LED devices had bright light at a constant current of 20 mA with CIE coordinates of (0.22, 0.26), (0.31, 0.40), (0.31, 0.43), (0.39, 0.44), (0.42, 0.32) and (0.40, 0.23), respectively. In addition, we tested the stability of CD@PVP under UV illumination, and the test results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CD@PVP did not significantly deteriorate after 5 h of continuous UV illumination (Figure S10). To measure the stability of LEDs devices, PL spectra were tested at different operating currents (20 mA, 40 mA, 60 mA, 80 mA, and 100 mA and at a voltage of 3 V). As shown in Figure S11, it can be observed that with the increase of the working current, there was no change in the EL spectrum and only an increase in the intensity of the EL spectrum. This indicated that the luminous intensity of LEDs had not reached saturation even at a working current of 100 mA. The rich color conversion demonstrated the great potential of multicolor CD-based LEDs for lighting applications.

Overall, in this study, full-color fluorescent CDs ranging from blue to red were directly synthesized using an acid catalyst synthesis strategy using mAP and oPD as reaction precursors. By adjusting the dosage of the acid catalyst used, the redshift of the fluorescence emission wavelength was tuned from 397 nm to 650 nm, and the corresponding particle size of the CDs was increased from 2.22 nm to 5.09 nm. XPS spectroscopy demonstrated that the corresponding graphite-N content was increased with the fluorescence shifted wavelength from blue to red, while the oxygen-containing functional groups exhibited a significant decrease. These results suggest that excessive acid addition may hinder the carbonization of CDs as well as the generation of graphite-N while introducing more oxygen-containing photoenergy groups finally leading to the luminescence blue shift. On this basis, we use the multicolor fluorescent properties of full-color fluorescent CDs as fluorescent inks for advanced information anti-counterfeiting and encryption, and their excellent optical properties in colorful LEDs. The present synthetic strategy is expected to provide favorable technical support for subsequent synthetic routes of multicolor fluorescent CDs and pave the way for future development of full-color fluorescent CDs.

Huajiang Hu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiurong Li: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Xiao Gong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2024.102154.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hola, K.; Sudolska, M.; Kalytchuk, S.; Nachtigallova, D.; Rogach, A. L.; Otyepka, M.; Zboril, R., Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots. ACS Nano 2017, 11 (12), 12402-12410.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao, M.; Zhao, X.; Gong, X., Ionic liquid-assisted fast synthesis of carbon dots with strong fluorescence and their tunable multicolor emission. Small 2022, 18 (11), e2106683.
